name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
ٱلْحَآقَّةُ (١)
```

```english
The Besieger! (1)
```

```arabic
مَا ٱلْحَآقَّةُ (٢)
```

```english
What is the Besieger?! (2)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلْحَآقَّةُ (٣)
```

```english
What will show you what is the Besieger?! (3)
```

```arabic
كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ وَعَادٌۢ بِٱلْقَارِعَةِ (٤)
```

```english
Thamudand ‘Ad denied the Cataclysm. (4)
```

```arabic
فَأَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَأُهْلِكُوا۟ بِٱلطَّاغِيَةِ (٥)
```

```english
As for Thamud, they were destroyed by the Cry. (5)
```

```arabic
وَأَمَّا عَادٌ فَأُهْلِكُوا۟ بِرِيحٍ صَرْصَرٍ عَاتِيَةٍ (٦)
```

```english
And as for ‘Ad, they were destroyed by a fierce icy gale, (6)
```

```arabic
سَخَّرَهَا عَلَيْهِمْ سَبْعَ لَيَالٍ وَثَمَـٰنِيَةَ أَيَّامٍ حُسُومًا فَتَرَى ٱلْقَوْمَ فِيهَا صَرْعَىٰ كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجَازُ نَخْلٍ خَاوِيَةٍ (٧)
```

```english
which He clamped on them for seven gruelling nights and eight days, so that you could see the people there lying about prostrate as if they were hollow trunks of palm trees. (7)
```

```arabic
فَهَلْ تَرَىٰ لَهُم مِّنۢ بَاقِيَةٍ (٨)
```

```english
So do you see any remaining trace of them? (8)
```

```arabic
وَجَآءَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَمَن قَبْلَهُۥ وَٱلْمُؤْتَفِكَـٰتُ بِٱلْخَاطِئَةِ (٩)
```

```english
Then Pharaoh and those who were before him, and the towns that were overturned, brought about iniquity. (9)
```

```arabic
فَعَصَوْا۟ رَسُولَ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَخَذَهُمْ أَخْذَةً رَّابِيَةً (١٠)
```

```english
They disobeyed the apostle of their Lord, so He seized them with a terrible seizing. (10)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا لَمَّا طَغَا ٱلْمَآءُ حَمَلْنَـٰكُمْ فِى ٱلْجَارِيَةِ (١١)
```

```english
Indeed when the Flood rose high, We carried you in a floating ark, (11)
```

```arabic
لِنَجْعَلَهَا لَكُمْ تَذْكِرَةً وَتَعِيَهَآ أُذُنٌ وَٰعِيَةٌ (١٢)
```

```english
that We might make it a reminder for you, and that receptive ears might remember it. (12)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah mentions the Inevitable (الحاقة) — *the Great Resurrection* — and has also referred to it as the Clatterer (القارعة) and the Imminent Event (الواقعة).

The discourse within it is organized into three sections: a section mentioning in brief the nations who denied it, whereupon Allah seized them with a surpassing punishment; a section describing the Inevitable and the division of people therein into the People of the Right and the People of the Left, along with the difference in their states of felicity and wretchedness; and a section emphasizing the truthfulness of the Quran in its foretelling of it, and that it is the Certain Truth (حق اليقين). The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of its verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الْحَاقَّةُ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ وَما أَدْراكَ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ** — **The Inevitable! What is the Inevitable? And what will show you what the Inevitable is?** (1-3). What is meant by the Inevitable (الحاقة) is the Great Resurrection. It is named thus because of its established certainty (ثبوتها), a reality that cannot be averted and in which there is no doubt; derived from the "truth" (حق) of a thing, meaning it is fixed and established with an actual reality (تقررا واقعيا).

The word "What" (مَا) in "What is the Inevitable?" (مَا الْحَاقَّةُ) is interrogative, serving to magnify its matter (تفخيم أمرها). For this very reason, the explicit noun was used in place of a pronoun; He did not say, "What is it?" The interrogative sentence serves as the predicate (خبر) for "The Inevitable" (الحاقة).

Thus, His saying: "The Inevitable! What is the Inevitable?" is intended to magnify the matter of the Resurrection, conveying its gravity and the greatness of its reality with repeated emphasis.

His saying: "And what will show you what the Inevitable is?" is an address negating the [ordinary] knowledge of the reality of that Day. This expression is an allusion (كناية) to the absolute importance of the matter and its reaching the ultimate degree of grandeur. Perhaps this is what is meant by what was narrated from Ibn Abbas: "Whatever occurs in the Quran of His saying, the Exalted: 'What will show you' (ما أَدْراكَ), He has indeed shown him; and whatever occurs of His saying: 'What will make you know' (ما يُدْرِيكَ), He has kept it hidden from him." This means that "What will show you" is an allusion, while "What will make you know" is an explicit statement.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ وَعادٌ بِالْقارِعَةِ** — **Thamud and ‘Ad denied the Clatterer.** (4). What is meant by the Clatterer (القارعة) is the Resurrection. It is named thus because it strikes (تقرع) and crushes the heavens and the earth by changing them, the mountains by setting them in motion, the sun by folding it up, the moon by eclipsing it, the stars by scattering them, and all things by its overwhelming power, as stated in the verses. The apparent requirement of the context would have been to say: "Thamud and ‘Ad denied it," but the "Clatterer" was placed in the position of the pronoun to emphasize the magnification of its matter.

This verse and those following it until the completion of nine verses, although intended to point briefly to the stories of the people of Noah, ‘Ad, Thamud, Pharaoh, those before him, and the overturned towns, and their destruction, are in reality an exposition of the Inevitable (الحاقة) through some of its attributes—namely, that Allah destroyed many nations for denying it. Thus, it is effectively an answer to the question posed by the interrogative "What," just as His saying: "Then when the Trumpet is blown..." etc., is another answer.

The summary of the meaning is: It is the Clatterer which was denied by Thamud, ‘Ad, Pharaoh, those before him, the overturned towns, and the people of Noah; so Allah seized them with a surpassing seizure and destroyed them with an extirpating punishment.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَأُهْلِكُوا بِالطَّاغِيَةِ** — **As for Thamud, they were destroyed by the Overwhelming [Cry].** (5). This is a detailed exposition of the consequence of their denial of the Clatterer.

What is meant by the Overwhelming (الطَّاغِيَةِ) is the Cry (الصيحة), the Earthquake (الرجفة), or the Thunderbolt (الصاعقة), according to the different outward expressions of the Quran regarding the cause of their destruction in their story. The Exalted said: **وَأَخَذَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا الصَّيْحَةُ** — "The Cry seized those who were wrongdoing" (Hud: 67); and He also said: **فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ** — "So the earthquake seized them" (Al-A'raf: 87); and He also said: **فَأَخَذَتْهُمْ صاعِقَةُ الْعَذابِ الْهُونِ** — "The thunderbolt of a humiliating punishment seized them" (Fussilat: 17).

It is also said that the Overwhelming (الطاغية) is a verbal noun (مصدر) like "transgression" (الطغيان) or "rebellion" (الطغوى), and the meaning would be: "As for Thamud, they were destroyed because of their transgression." This is supported by His saying, the Exalted: **كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ بِطَغْواها** — "Thamud denied [the truth] because of their rebellion" (Ash-Shams: 11).

However, the first of the two views is more appropriate to the context of the subsequent verses, as they are presented to explain the manner of their destruction—whether by the wind, the surpassing seizure, or the transgression of the water. Therefore, the destruction of Thamud by the "Overwhelming" should refer to the manner of their destruction.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَمَّا عادٌ فَأُهْلِكُوا بِرِيحٍ صَرْصَرٍ عاتِيَةٍ** — **And as for ‘Ad, they were destroyed by a fierce, icy gale.** (6). The "fierce gale" (الصرصر) is a cold wind with a powerful blast, and "icy" (عاتية) is from "insolence" (العتو), meaning transgression and departure from obedience and suitability.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **سَخَّرَها عَلَيْهِمْ سَبْعَ لَيالٍ وَثَمانِيَةَ أَيَّامٍ حُسُوماً فَتَرَى الْقَوْمَ فِيها صَرْعى كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجازُ نَخْلٍ خاوِيَةٍ** — **which He let loose on them for seven nights and eight days consecutively, so that you could see the people therein lying prostrate as if they were hollow trunks of palm trees.** (7). Letting it loose (تسخيرها) upon them means giving it power over them. "Consecutively" (الحسوم) is the plural of "decisive" (حاسم), like "witnesses" (شهود) is the plural of "witness" (شاهد), derived from "severing" (الحسم) in the sense of repeating cauterization several times in succession. It is an adjective for the "seven," meaning seven nights and eight days in continuous succession. "Prostrate" (صرعى) is the plural of "fallen" (صريع). "Trunks" (أعجاز) is the plural of "trunk" (عجز), the end of a thing. "Hollow" (خاوية) means empty inside and cast down. The meaning is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَهَلْ تَرى لَهُمْ مِنْ باقِيَةٍ** — **Do you see any of them remaining?** (8). That is, any soul remaining. The sentence is an allusion to the total destruction that encompassed them all. It is also said that "remaining" (الباقية) is a verbal noun meaning "survival" (البقاء), intended here as "remnant" (البقية). The meaning we previously mentioned is closer.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَجاءَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَمَنْ قَبْلَهُ وَالْمُؤْتَفِكاتُ بِالْخاطِئَةِ** — **Pharaoh and those who were before him and the overturned towns committed sins.** (9). What is meant by Pharaoh is the Pharaoh of Moses; by "those before him," the nations that preceded him in time among the deniers; and by "the overturned towns" (المؤتفكات), the villages of the people of Lot and the community residing therein. "Sin" (خاطئة) is a verbal noun meaning "error" (الخطاء), and "committing sin" refers to erring from the path of servitude (العبودية). The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَعَصَوْا رَسُولَ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَخَذَهُمْ أَخْذَةً رابِيَةً** — **And they disobeyed the apostle of their Lord, so He seized them with a surpassing seizure.** (10). The pronoun in "they disobeyed" (فَعَصَوْا) refers to Pharaoh, those before him, and the overturned towns. What is meant by "the apostle" is the genus. "Surpassing" (الرابية) means increasing, from "it increased" (ربا يربو ربوة) when it grows. What is meant by the "surpassing seizure" is a severe punishment. It is also said: a punishment exceeding all other punishments; and it is said: one that breaks the norm.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّا لَمَّا طَغَى الْماءُ حَمَلْناكُمْ فِي الْجارِيَةِ** — **Indeed when the water overflowed, We carried you in the floating [ark],** (11). This is an allusion to the flood of Noah, and the "floating" (الجارية) is the ship. Those addressed are considered as having been carried in the ship of Noah, though those actually carried were their ancestors, because they all constitute a single species, and the state of some is attributed to the whole. The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لِنَجْعَلَها لَكُمْ تَذْكِرَةً وَتَعِيَها أُذُنٌ واعِيَةٌ** — **that We may make it a reminder for you, and that retentive ears may retain it.** (12). This is the rationale for carrying them in the ship. Thus, the pronoun in "that We may make it" (لِنَجْعَلَها) refers to the act of carrying, considering it His act—meaning: We performed that act for you so that We might make it a matter by which you remember, a lesson by which you take heed, and an exhortation by which you are admonished.

His saying: "and that retentive ears may retain it" (وَتَعِيَها أُذُنٌ واعِيَةٌ). "Retaining" (الوعي) is to place something in a vessel. What is meant by the ear "retaining" it is its being established in the soul and preserved therein, so that its benefit—which is remembrance and taking heed—may follow.

In the verse, with both its clauses, there is an allusion to Divine guidance (الهداية الربوبية) in both its types: guidance in the sense of "showing the way" (إراءة الطريق) and guidance in the sense of "conveying to the goal" (الإيصال إلى المطلوب).

To clarify: It is part of the general Divine Sunnah (السنة الربوبية) — *the established way of Allah* — prevailing in the cosmos to guide every species of creation toward its appropriate perfection according to its specific existence, by equipping it with what drives it toward its end. This is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى** — "He who gave everything its creation and then guided it" (Ta-Ha: 50), and His saying: **الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدى** — "He who created and proportioned, and who determined and guided" (Al-A'la: 2-3). The clarification of this has already been provided in the commentary on Surah Ta-Ha, Surah Al-A'la, and others.

Man shares with other material species in having a generative perfection (استكمال تكويني) and an existential journey toward his existential perfection through Divine guidance that drives him toward his required end. He is distinguished among them by legislative perfection (الاستكمال التشريعي); for the human soul attains perfection through its voluntary actions, by the attributes and qualities that attach to it and the dispositions (الملكات) and states it assumes in the life of this world. This is the purpose of man's existence, by which he lives an eternal, felicitous life.

This is the reason necessitating the legislation of the religious way (السنة الدينية) by sending messengers and revealing books, and guiding toward them, **لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَى اللهِ حُجَّةٌ بَعْدَ الرُّسُلِ** — "so that mankind may not have any argument against Allah after the apostles" (An-Nisa: 165). This was detailed in the discussions on Prophethood in the second volume of this book and elsewhere. This is guidance in the sense of "showing the way" and making known the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — which man cannot but traverse. The Exalted said: **إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَإِمَّا كَفُوراً** — "Indeed We have guided him to the way, be he grateful or ungrateful" (Al-Insan: 3). If he adheres to the path and traverses it, he lives a good and felicitous life; if he abandons it and turns away, he perishes in eternal wretchedness, and the proof is established against him in any case. The Exalted said: **لِيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ وَيَحْيى مَنْ حَيَّ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ** — "so that he who perishes might perish by a clear proof, and he who lives might live by a clear proof" (Al-Anfal: 42).

Once this is established, it becomes clear that it is part of the Divine Sunnah to guide people to the felicity of their lives by showing the way leading to it; this is alluded to by His saying: "that We may make it a reminder for you" (لِنَجْعَلَها لَكُمْ تَذْكِرَةً). For a reminder does not necessitate remembrance from the one reminded; it may take effect or it may fail to do so.

It is also part of the Divine Sunnah to guide things to their perfections in the sense of bringing them to their conclusion and conveying them to it by moving them and driving them toward it. This is alluded to by His saying: "and that retentive ears may retain it" (وَتَعِيَها أُذُنٌ واعِيَةٌ). For the mentioned "retaining" is among the instances of being guided by Divine guidance. The reason the Exalted did not attribute the "retaining" to Himself as He attributed the "reminder" to Himself is that what is required by the reminder is the completion of the proof (إتمام الحجة), which is from Allah. As for the "retaining," although it is attributed to Him just as it is attributed to man, the context is one of invitation and the mention of reward and recompense for answering the invitation. Reward and recompense are among the effects of "retaining" insofar as it is an act of man attributed to him, not insofar as it is attributed to Allah, the Exalted.

It appears from the noble verse that external events have an influence on the actions of man, just as it appears from verses like His saying: **وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ** — "If the people of the towns had believed and been God-conscious, We would have opened for them blessings from the heaven and the earth" (Al-A'raf: 96), that the actions of man have an influence on external events. Some discussion on this has already preceded.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, it is narrated that Ibn al-Mundhir recorded from Ibn Jurayj regarding His saying: "that We may make it a reminder for you" (لِنَجْعَلَها لَكُمْ تَذْكِرَةً), he said: "For the Ummah of Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. How many a ship has perished and how many a trace has vanished—meaning what remained of the ship until the Ummah of Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله reached it—so they saw it; its planks used to be seen on [Mount] Judi."

**I say:** What supports this has already preceded in the story of Noah in the commentary of Surah Hud.

In the same source, it is narrated that Sa'id ibn Mansur, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh recorded from Makhul, who said: "When 'and that retentive ears may retain it' (وَتَعِيَها أُذُنٌ واعِيَةٌ) was revealed, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'I asked my Lord to make it the ear of Ali.'" Makhul said: "Thus, Ali used to say: 'I never heard anything from the Messenger of Allah and then forgot it.'"

In the same source, it is narrated that Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Wahidi, Ibn Mardawayh, Ibn ‘Asakir, and Ibn al-Najari recorded from Buraydah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to Ali: 'Indeed, Allah has commanded me to draw you near and not distance you, and to teach you so that you may retain, and it is right for you to retain.' Then this verse was revealed: 'and that retentive ears may retain it' (وَتَعِيَها أُذُنٌ واعِيَةٌ)."

In the same source, Abu Nu'aym recorded in *al-Hilyah* from Ali, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'O Ali, indeed Allah has commanded me to draw you near and teach you so that you may retain.' Then this verse was revealed: 'and that retentive ears may retain it' (وَتَعِيَها أُذُنٌ واعِيَةٌ). So you are the retentive ear for my knowledge."

**I say:** This meaning is also narrated in *Tafsir al-Burhan* from Sa'd ibn Abdullah with his chain of narration from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام, and from al-Kulayni with his chain from him عليه‌السلام, and from Ibn Babawayh with his chain from Jabir from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام.

It was also narrated by Ibn Shahrashub from *Hilyat al-Awliya* from Umar ibn Ali; and by al-Wahidi in *Asbab al-Nuzul* from Buraydah; and by Abu al-Qasim ibn Habib in his *Tafsir* from Zirr ibn Hubaysh from Ali عليه‌السلام.

In *Ghayat al-Maram*, sixteen hadiths have been narrated in this regard from the paths of both groups [Sunni and Shi'a], and it is stated in *al-Burhan* that Muhammad ibn al-Abbas narrated thirty hadiths therein from the paths of the general public (العامة) and the specific group (الخاصة).
